html:
IV

I

Vernon woke, after a long time, to find himself in bed. It was, of course, the natural place to be when you woke up, but what wasnвАЩt natural was to have a great hump sticking up in front of you in the bed. It was whilst he was staring at this that someone spoke to him. That someone was Dr.¬†Coles, whom Vernon knew quite well.

вАЬWell, well,вАЭ said Dr.¬†Coles, вАЬand how are we feeling?вАЭ

Vernon didnвАЩt know how Dr.¬†Coles was feeling. He himself was feeling rather sick and said so.

вАЬI dare say, I dare say,вАЭ said Dr.¬†Coles.

вАЬAnd I think I hurt somewhere,вАЭ said Vernon. вАЬI think I hurt very much.вАЭ

вАЬI dare say, I dare say,вАЭ said Dr.¬†Coles againвБ†вАФnot very helpfully, Vernon thought.

вАЬPerhaps IвАЩd feel better if I got up,вАЭ said Vernon. вАЬCan I get up?вАЭ

вАЬNot just now, IвАЩm afraid,вАЭ said the doctor. вАЬYou see, youвАЩve had a fall.вАЭ

вАЬYes,вАЭ said Vernon. вАЬThe Beast came after me.вАЭ

вАЬEh? WhatвАЩs that? The Beast? What Beast?вАЭ

вАЬNothing,вАЭ said Vernon.

вАЬA dog, I expect,вАЭ said the doctor. вАЬJumped at the wall and barked. You mustnвАЩt be afraid of dogs, my boy.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm not,вАЭ said Vernon.

вАЬAnd what were you doing so far from home, eh? No business to be where you were.вАЭ

вАЬNobody told me not to,вАЭ said Vernon.

вАЬHum, hum, I wonder. Well, IвАЩm afraid youвАЩve got to take your punishment. Do you know, youвАЩve broken your leg, my boy?вАЭ

вАЬHave I?вАЭ Vernon was gratifiedвБ†вАФenchanted. He had broken his leg. He felt very important.

вАЬYes, youвАЩll have to lie here for a bit, and then it will mean crutches for a while. Do you know what crutches are?вАЭ

Yes, Vernon knew. Mr.¬†Jobber, the blacksmithвАЩs father, had crutches. And he was to have crutches! How wonderful!

вАЬCan I try them now?вАЭ

Dr.¬†Coles laughed.

вАЬSo you like the idea? No, IвАЩm afraid youвАЩll have to wait a bit. And you must try and be a brave boy, you know. And then youвАЩll get well quicker.вАЭ

вАЬThank you,вАЭ said Vernon politely. вАЬI donвАЩt think I do feel very well. Can you take this funny thing out of my bed? I think it would be more comfortable then.вАЭ

But it seemed that the funny thing was called a cradle, and that it couldnвАЩt be taken away. And it seemed, too, that Vernon would not be able to move about in bed because his leg was all tied up to a long piece of wood. And suddenly it didnвАЩt seem a very nice thing to have a broken leg after all.

VernonвАЩs underlip trembled a little. He was not going to cryвБ†вАФno, he was a big boy and big boys didnвАЩt cry. Nurse said so. And then he knew that he wanted NurseвБ†вАФwanted her badly. He wanted her reassuring presence, her omniscience, her creaking rustling majesty.

вАЬSheвАЩll be coming back soon,вАЭ said Dr.¬†Coles. вАЬYes, soon. In the meantime, this nurse is going to look after youвБ†вАФNurse Frances.вАЭ

Nurse Frances moved into VernonвАЩs range of vision and Vernon studied her in silence. She too was starched and crackling; that was all to the good. But she wasnвАЩt big like Nurse. She was thinner than MummyвБ†вАФas thin as Aunt Nina. He wasnвАЩt sureвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

And then he met her eyesвБ†вАФsteady eyes, more green than grey, and he felt, as most people felt, that with Nurse Frances things would be вАЬall right.вАЭ

She smiled at himвБ†вАФbut not in the way that visitors smiled. It was a grave smile, friendly but reserved.

вАЬIвАЩm sorry you feel sick,вАЭ she said. вАЬWould you like some orange juice?вАЭ

Vernon considered the matter and said he thought he would. Dr.¬†Coles went out of the room and Nurse Frances brought him the orange juice in a most curious-looking cup with a long spout. And it appeared that Vernon was to drink from the spout.

It made him laugh, but laughing hurt him, and so he stopped. Nurse Frances suggested he should go to sleep again, but he said he didnвАЩt want to go to sleep.

вАЬThen I shouldnвАЩt go to sleep,вАЭ said Nurse Frances. вАЬI wonder if you can count how many irises there are on that wall? You can start on the right side, and IвАЩll start on the left side. You can count, canвАЩt you?вАЭ

Vernon said proudly that he could count up to a hundred.

вАЬThat is a lot,вАЭ said Nurse Frances. вАЬThere arenвАЩt nearly as many irises as a hundred. I guess there are seventy-five. Now what do you guess?вАЭ

Vernon guessed that there were fifty. There couldnвАЩt, he felt sure, possibly be more than that. He began to count, but somehow, without knowing it, his eyelids closed and he slept.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

II

NoiseвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ noise and painвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He woke with a start. He felt hot, very hot and there was a pain all down one side. And the noise was coming nearer. It was the noise that one always connected with MummyвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

She came into the room like a whirlwind, a kind of cloak affair she wore swinging out behind her. She was like a birdвБ†вАФa great big birdвБ†вАФand like a bird, she swooped down upon him.

вАЬVernonвБ†вАФmy darlingвБ†вАФMummyвАЩs own darling! What have they done to you? How awful! How terrible! My child!вАЭ

She was crying. Vernon began to cry too. He was suddenly frightened. Myra was moaning and weeping.

вАЬMy little child. All I have in the world. God, donвАЩt take him from me. DonвАЩt take him from me! If he dies, I shall die too!вАЭ

вАЬMrs.¬†DeyreвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬVernonвБ†вАФVernonвБ†вАФmy babyвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬMrs.¬†DeyreвБ†вАФplease!вАЭ

There was crisp command in the voice rather than appeal.

вАЬPlease donвАЩt touch him. You will hurt him.вАЭ

вАЬHurt him? I? His mother?вАЭ

вАЬYou donвАЩt seem to realize, Mrs.¬†Deyre, that his leg is broken. I must ask you, please, to leave the room.вАЭ

вАЬYouвАЩre hiding something from me. Tell meвБ†вАФtell meвБ†вАФwill the leg have to be amputated?вАЭ

A wail came from Vernon. He had not the least idea what вАЬamputatedвАЭ meant, but it sounded painfulвБ†вАФand more than painful, terrifying. His wail broke into a scream.

вАЬHeвАЩs dying!вАЭ cried Myra. вАЬHeвАЩs dyingвБ†вАФand they wonвАЩt tell me. But he shall die in my arms.вАЭ

вАЬMrs.¬†DeyreвБ†вАФвАЭ

Somehow Nurse Frances had got between his mother and the bed. She was holding his mother by the shoulder. Her voice had the tone that NurseвАЩs had had when speaking to Katie, the under-housemaid.

вАЬMrs.¬†Deyre, listen to me. You must control yourself. You must!вАЭ Then she looked up. VernonвАЩs father was standing in the doorway. вАЬMr.¬†Deyre, please take your wife away. I cannot have my patient excited and upset.вАЭ

His father noddedвБ†вАФa quiet understanding nod. He just looked at Vernon once and said: вАЬBad luck, old chap. I broke an arm once.вАЭ

The world became suddenly less terrifying. Other people broke legs and arms. His father had hold of his motherвАЩs shoulder, he was leading her towards the door, speaking to her in a low voice. She was protesting, arguing, her voice high and shrill with emotion.

вАЬHow can you understand? YouвАЩve never cared for the child like I have. It takes a mother. How can I leave my child to be looked after by a stranger? He needs his mother.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ You donвАЩt understand. I love him. ThereвАЩs nothing like a motherвАЩs careвБ†вАФeveryone says so.вАЭ

вАЬVernon darlingвАЭвБ†вАФshe broke from her husbandвАЩs clasp, came back towards the bedвБ†вАФвАЬyou want me, donвАЩt you? You want Mummy?вАЭ

вАЬI want Nurse,вАЭ sobbed Vernon. вАЬI want Nurse.вАЭ

He meant his own Nurse, not Nurse Frances.

вАЬOh!вАЭ said Myra. She stood there quivering.

вАЬCome, my dear,вАЭ said VernonвАЩs father gently. вАЬCome away.вАЭ

She leant against him, and together they passed from the room. Faint words floated back into the room.

вАЬMy own child, to turn from me to a stranger.вАЭ

Nurse Frances smoothed the sheet and suggested a drink of water.

вАЬNurse is coming back very soon,вАЭ she said. вАЬWeвАЩll write to her today, shall we? You shall tell me what to say.вАЭ

A queer new feeling surged over VernonвБ†вАФa sort of odd gratitude. Somebody had actually understood.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

III

When Vernon, later, was to look back upon his childhood, this one period was to stand out quite clearly from the rest. вАЬThe time I broke my legвАЭ marked a distinct era.

He was to appreciate, too, various small incidents that were accepted by him at the time as a matter of course. For instance, a rather stormy interview that took place between Dr.¬†Coles and his mother. Naturally this did not take place in VernonвАЩs sick room, but MyraвАЩs raised voice penetrated closed doors. Vernon heard indignant exclamations of вАЬI donвАЩt know what you mean by upsetting him.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I consider I ought to nurse my own child.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Naturally I was distressedвБ†вАФIвАЩm not one of these people who simply have no heartвБ†вАФno heart at all. Look at WalterвБ†вАФnever turned a hair!вАЭ

There were many skirmishes, too, not to say pitched battles fought between Myra and Nurse Frances. In these cases Nurse Frances always won, but at a certain cost. Myra Deyre was wildly and furiously jealous of what she called вАЬthe paid nurse.вАЭ She was forced to submit to Dr.¬†ColeвАЩs dictums, but she did so with a bad grace and with an overt rudeness that Nurse Frances never seemed to notice.

In after years Vernon remembered nothing of the pain and tedium that there must have been. He only remembered happy days of playing and talking as he had never played and talked before. For in Nurse Frances, he found a grown up who didnвАЩt think things вАЬfunnyвАЭ or вАЬquaint.вАЭ Somebody who listened sensibly and who made serious and sensible suggestions. To Nurse Frances he was able to speak of Poodle, Squirrel and Tree, and of Mr.¬†Green and the hundred children. And instead of saying вАЬWhat a funny game!вАЭ Nurse Frances merely inquired whether the hundred children were girls or boysвБ†вАФan aspect of the matter which Vernon had never thought of before. But he and Nurse Frances decided that there were fifty of each, which seemed a very fair arrangement.

If sometimes, off his guard, he played his make-believe games aloud, Nurse Frances never seemed to notice or to think it unusual. She had the same calm comfortableness of old Nurse about her, but she had something that mattered far more to Vernon, the gift of answering questionsвБ†вАФand he knew, instinctively, that the answers were always true. Sometimes she would say: вАЬI donвАЩt know that myself,вАЭ or вАЬYou must ask someone else. IвАЩm not clever enough to tell you that.вАЭ There was no pretence of omniscience about her.

Sometimes, after tea, she would tell Vernon stories. The stories were never the same two days runningвБ†вАФone day they would be about naughty little boys and girls, and the next day they would be about enchanted princesses. Vernon liked the latter kind best. There was one in particular that he loved, about a princess in a tower with golden hair and a vagabond prince in a ragged green hat. The story ended up in a forest and it was possibly for that reason that Vernon liked it so much.

Sometimes there would be an extra listener. Myra used to come in and be with Vernon during the early afternoon when Nurse Frances had her time off, but VernonвАЩs father used sometimes to come in after tea when the stories were going on. Little by little it became a habit. Walter Deyre would sit in the shadows just behind Nurse FrancesвАЩ chair, and from there he would watch, not his child, but the storyteller. One day Vernon saw his fatherвАЩs hand steal out and close gently over Nurse FrancesвАЩ wrist.

And then something happened which surprised him very much. Nurse Frances got up from her chair.

вАЬIвАЩm afraid we must turn you out for this evening, Mr.¬†Deyre,вАЭ she said quietly. вАЬVernon and I have things to do.вАЭ

This astonished Vernon very much, because he couldnвАЩt think what those things were. He was still more puzzled when his father got up also and said in a low voice:

вАЬI beg your pardon.вАЭ

Nurse Frances bent her head a little, but remained standing. Her eyes met Walter DeyreвАЩs steadily. He said quietly:

вАЬWill you believe that I am really sorry, and let me come tomorrow?вАЭ

After that, in some way that Vernon could not have defined, his fatherвАЩs manner was different. He no longer sat so near Nurse Frances. He talked more to Vernon and occasionally they all three played a gameвБ†вАФusually Old Maid for which Vernon had a wild passion. They were happy evenings enjoyed by all three.

One day when Nurse Frances was out of the room, Walter Deyre said abruptly:

вАЬDo you like that nurse of yours, Vernon?вАЭ

вАЬNurse Frances? I like her lots. DonвАЩt you, Father?вАЭ

вАЬYes,вАЭ said Walter Deyre, вАЬI do.вАЭ

There was a sadness in his voice which Vernon felt.

вАЬIs anything the matter, Father?вАЭ

вАЬNothing that can be put right. The horse that gets left at the post never has much chance of making goodвБ†вАФand the fact that itвАЩs the horseвАЩs own fault doesnвАЩt make matters any better. But thatвАЩs double Dutch to you, old man. Anyway, enjoy your Nurse Frances while youвАЩve got her. There arenвАЩt many of her sort knocking about.вАЭ

And then Nurse Frances came back and they played Animal Grab.

But Walter DeyreвАЩs words had set VernonвАЩs mind to work. He tackled Nurse Frances next morning.

вАЬArenвАЩt you going to be here always?вАЭ

вАЬNo. Only till you get wellвБ†вАФor nearly well.вАЭ

вАЬWonвАЩt you stay always? IвАЩd like you to.вАЭ

вАЬBut you see, thatвАЩs not my work. My work is to look after people who are ill.вАЭ

вАЬDo you like doing that?вАЭ

вАЬYes, very much.вАЭ

вАЬWhy?вАЭ

вАЬWell, you see, everyone has some particular kind of work that they like doing and that suits them.вАЭ

вАЬMummy hasnвАЩt.вАЭ

вАЬOh, yes, she has. Her work is to look after this big house and see that everything goes right, and to take care of you and your father.вАЭ

вАЬFather was a soldier once. He told me that if ever there was a war, heвАЩd go and be a soldier again.вАЭ

вАЬAre you very fond of your father, Vernon?вАЭ

вАЬI love Mummy best, of course. Mummy says little boys always love their mothers best. I like being with Father, of course, but thatвАЩs different. I expect itвАЩs because heвАЩs a man. What shall I be when I grow up, do you think? I want to be a sailor.вАЭ

вАЬPerhaps youвАЩll write books.вАЭ

вАЬWhat about?вАЭ

Nurse Frances smiled a little.

вАЬPerhaps about Mr.¬†Green, and Poodle and Squirrel and Tree.вАЭ

вАЬBut everyone would say that that was silly.вАЭ

вАЬLittle boys wouldnвАЩt think so. And besides, when you grow up, you will have different people in your headвБ†вАФlike Mr.¬†Green and the children, only grown-up people. And then you could write about them.вАЭ

Vernon thought for a long time, then he shook his head.

вАЬI think IвАЩll be a soldier like Father. Most of the Deyres have been soldiers, Mummy says. Of course you have to be very brave to be a soldier, but I think I would be brave enough.вАЭ

Nurse Frances was silent a moment. She was thinking of what Walter Deyre had said of his small son.

вАЬHeвАЩs a plucky little chapвБ†вАФabsolutely fearless. DoesnвАЩt know what fear is! You should see him on his pony.вАЭ

Yes, Vernon was fearless enough in one sense. He had the power of endurance, too. He had borne the pain and discomfort of his broken leg unusually well for so young a child.

But there was another kind of fear. She said slowly after a minute or two:

вАЬTell me again how you fell off the wall that day.вАЭ

She knew all about the Beast, and had been careful to display no ridicule. She listened now to Vernon and as he finished she said gently:

вАЬBut youвАЩve known for quite a long time, havenвАЩt you, that it isnвАЩt a real Beast? That itвАЩs only a thing made of wood and wires.вАЭ

вАЬI do know,вАЭ said Vernon. вАЬBut I donвАЩt dream it like that. And when I saw it in the garden coming at meвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬYou ran awayвБ†вАФwhich was rather a pity, wasnвАЩt it? It would have been much better to have stayed and looked. Then youвАЩd have seen the men, and would have known just what it was. ItвАЩs always a good thing to look. Then you can run away afterwards if you still want toвБ†вАФbut you usually donвАЩt. And Vernon, IвАЩll tell you something else.вАЭ

вАЬYes?вАЭ

вАЬThings are never so frightening in front of you as they are behind you. Remember that. Anything seems frightening when itвАЩs behind your back and you canвАЩt see it. ThatвАЩs why itвАЩs always better to turn and face thingsвБ†вАФand then very often you find they are nothing at all.вАЭ

Vernon said thoughtfully: вАЬIf IвАЩd turned round I wouldnвАЩt have broken my leg, would I?вАЭ

вАЬNo.вАЭ

Vernon sighed.

вАЬI donвАЩt mind having broken my leg very much. It has been very nice having you to play with.вАЭ

He thought Nurse Frances murmured вАЬPoor childвАЭ under her breath, but that, of course, was absurd. She said smiling:

вАЬIвАЩve enjoyed it too. Some of my ill people donвАЩt like to play.вАЭ

вАЬYou really do like playing, donвАЩt you?вАЭ said Vernon. вАЬSo does Mr.¬†Green.вАЭ

He added rather stiffly, for he felt shy:

вАЬPlease donвАЩt go away very soon, will you?вАЭ

IV

But as it happened, Nurse Frances went away much sooner than she might have done. It all happened very suddenly, as things in VernonвАЩs experience always did.

It started very simplyвБ†вАФsomething that Myra offered to do for Vernon and that he said he would rather have done by Nurse Frances.

He was on crutches now for a short and painful time every day, enjoying the novelty of it very much. He soon got tired, however, and was ready to go back to bed. Today his mother had suggested his doing so, saying she would help him. But Vernon had been helped by her before. Those big white hands of hers were strangely clumsy. They hurt where they meant to help. He shrank from her well-meant efforts. He said he would wait for Nurse Frances, who never hurt.

The words came out with the tactless honesty of children, and in a minute Myra Deyre was at white heat.

Nurse Frances came in two or three minutes later to be received with a flood of reproach.

Turning the boy against his own motherвБ†вАФcruelвБ†вАФwicked. They were all alikeвБ†вАФeveryone was against her. She had nothing in the world but Vernon and now he was being turned against her too.

So it went onвБ†вАФa ceaseless stream. Nurse Frances bore it patiently enough without surprise or rancour. Mrs.¬†Deyre, she knew, was that kind of woman. Scenes were a relief to her. And hard words, Nurse Frances reflected with grim humour, can only harm if the utterer is dear to you. She was sorry for Myra Deyre for she realized how much real unhappiness and misery lay behind these hysterical outbursts.

It was an unfortunate moment for Walter Deyre to choose to enter the nursery. For a moment or two he stood surprised, then he flushed angrily.

вАЬReally, Myra, IвАЩm ashamed of you. You donвАЩt know what youвАЩre saying.вАЭ

She turned on him furiously.

вАЬI know what IвАЩm saying well enough. And I know what youвАЩve been doing. Slinking in here every dayвБ†вАФIвАЩve seen you. Always making love to some woman or other. Nursemaids, hospital nursesвБ†вАФitвАЩs all one to you.вАЭ

вАЬMyraвБ†вАФbe quiet!вАЭ

He was really angry now. Myra Deyre felt a throb of fear. But she hurled her last piece of invective.

вАЬYouвАЩre all alike, you hospital nurses. Flirting with other womenвАЩs husbands. You ought to be ashamed of yourself. Before the innocent child tooвБ†вАФputting all sorts of things into his head. But youвАЩll go out of my house. Yes, youвАЩll go right outвБ†вАФand I shall tell Dr.¬†Coles what I think of you.вАЭ

вАЬWould you mind continuing this edifying scene elsewhere?вАЭ Her husbandвАЩs voice was as she hated it mostвБ†вАФcold and sneering. вАЬHardly judicious in front of your innocent child, is it? I apologize, Nurse, for what my wife has been saying. Come, Myra.вАЭ

She wentвБ†вАФbeginning to cryвБ†вАФweakly frightened at what she had done. As usual, she had said more than she meant.

вАЬYouвАЩre cruel,вАЭ she sobbed. вАЬCruel. YouвАЩd like me to be dead. You hate me.вАЭ

She followed him out of the room. Nurse Frances put Vernon to bed. He wanted to ask questions but she talked of a dog, a big St.¬†Bernard, that she had had when she was a little girl and he was so much interested that he forgot everything else.

Much later that evening, VernonвАЩs father came to the nursery. He looked white and ill. Nurse Frances rose and came to where he stood in the doorway.

вАЬI donвАЩt know what to say. How can I apologizeвБ†вАФthe things my wife saidвБ†вАФвАЭ

Nurse Frances replied in a quiet matter-of-fact voice:

вАЬOh! itвАЩs quite all right. I understand. I think, though, that I had better go as soon as it can be arranged. My being here makes Mrs.¬†Deyre unhappy, and then she works herself up.вАЭ

вАЬIf she knew how wide of the mark her wild accusations are. That she should insult youвБ†вАФвАЭ

Nurse Frances laughedвБ†вАФnot, perhaps, very convincingly.

вАЬI always think itвАЩs absurd when people complain about being insulted,вАЭ she said cheerfully. вАЬSuch a pompous word, isnвАЩt it? Please donвАЩt worry or think I mind. You know, Mr.¬†Deyre, your wife isвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬYes?вАЭ

Her voice changed. It was grave and sad.

вАЬA very unhappy and lonely woman.вАЭ

вАЬDo you think that is entirely my fault?вАЭ

There was a pause. She lifted her eyesвБ†вАФthose steady green eyes.

вАЬYes,вАЭ she said, вАЬI do.вАЭ

He drew a long breath.

вАЬNo one else but you would have said that to me. YouвБ†вАФI suppose itвАЩs courage in you that I admire so muchвБ†вАФyour absolute fearless honesty. IвАЩm sorry for Vernon that he should lose you before he need.вАЭ

She said gravely: вАЬDonвАЩt blame yourself for things you neednвАЩt. This has not been your fault.вАЭ

вАЬNurse Frances.вАЭ It was Vernon, eagerly from bed. вАЬI donвАЩt want you to go away. DonвАЩt go away, pleaseвБ†вАФnot tonight.вАЭ

вАЬOf course not,вАЭ said Nurse Frances. вАЬWeвАЩve got to talk to Dr.¬†Coles about it.вАЭ

Nurse Frances left three days later. Vernon wept bitterly. He had lost the first real friend he had ever had.
